speaker: interviewee
text: Working as a carpenter, and the political situation in Israel was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was deteriorating.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was deteriorating.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because remember, I got my discharge in 1946. And '46, '47, there was really, the United Nations decided to do a partition. And eventually, in May of 1948, the-- the-- the state of Israel was born on May the 15th, which is another day I will never forget, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, in the meantime, I was a discharged soldier, right? All discharged soldiers joined the Haganah, which was the underground Israeli army. But in the meantime, all I did at that time, during those up-- those times when there were these-- were these unrest, we did just local guard duty in our area.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had four rifles for our area, but we were not allowed-- we had four rifles. I think we had four rounds per rifle, and we were not allowed to use these-- we were not allowed to fire the rifle. What is the purpose of a rifle if you're not allowed to fire it, I don't know. Because if you fired the rifle, you'll hear it, and then the British police would come and take it away.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, and I was deferred. You see, I was called up to the-- to the-- to the full-time duty at the beginning of '48-- yeah, in February or March. But since I was an only son, [NON-ENGLISH]. There was-- there was a regulation at the time, [NON-ENGLISH] are deferred. OK? So I was deferred.

speaker: interviewee
text: But on 15th of May, all deferments were canceled because there was an-- there was an emergency situation, because the Arab states were invading from all sides. So on the-- on that particular day, there-- there was a sign-- there were-- there were placards all over the place, all these deferred. And I had to report to a certain place. And I left together with a friend of mine, for Tel Aviv, on a bus. I arrived in Tel Aviv. From Tel Aviv, we were sent to a place called Tel Litwinsky, which was a former huge British army camp. And there, we were standing all in one long line. And on the same day, there was an air attack on the-- on the-- on the camp, and there were two Egyptian spitfires, they were-- they were machine gunning our camp. And I was watching those machine-- machine gunning our camp. Those are very interesting. But I-- I wasn't afraid somehow. Anyway, they were machine gunning our camp, and they left. And then there was an officer going along the line. It was an officer because he had the sign which said [NON-ENGLISH]. It didn't have any insignia. At that time, there were no insignia. You had to add an armband, it said [NON-ENGLISH], or whatever or [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means officer, [NON-ENGLISH] means sergeant. So this one was [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was from South Africa. He was-- he was a regular soldier, apparently.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he spoke with everybody, and he-- there was a-- my-- my friend next to me was a very tall, good looking fellow. He says, he says to him, 'you been in the army before?' He says, yes. 'What army?' He was Jewish-- he was also in the Jewish brigade group. He says, Jewish brigade. Ah, he says. Step aside. So he stepped aside, and all these people-- he took about 30 people. They-- they all left. And-- and my friend was-- two weeks later, he was-- he was killed in the Battle of Latrun, if you-- if you heard about that. That was an unfortunate battle where many people were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- then another one came and said, 'everybody who is an electrician, carpenter, automotive mechanic, and this, and step forward. So since I was a carpenter, I stepped forward.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, like, OK, he says. 'All come with us.' So we were about 30 or 40 people. We all went on attack, and we left. And we joined-- and we joined-- at this particular time, we joined the engineering corps. Now, what was the engineering corps? As I found out, I was not that happy about it. The engineering corps was mostly laying and dismantling of mines, which is a very dangerous and nasty job. And everybody was really-- we were not very happy that we had volunteered for this particular job, you know what I mean? But after-- we-- we-- we took this course at an engineering school. And after a while, we got used to that, you know? If you know what you're doing, and you do what-- you do exactly things according to the book, then there's very little chance of anything will happen to you. You do exactly the way it's supposed to be done, and you're going to be all right. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what we did. I joined the engineering corps, and eventually I-- I became part of a platoon, of an engineering platoon. And we started to lay mines left and right and start dismantling mines. And nothing ever happened except once, because somebody did something stupid he was not supposed to do. You know, those mines, there are-- there are anti-personnel mines, and there are anti-tank mines. The anti-tank mines, if a tank or a-- a truck goes over it, it blows up. But if a person stands-- steps on it, nothing happens because it needs a heavy weight. Now, the anti-personnel mines are different. They have a-- the mine is buried underground, and then there's a thin wire. And if you trip the wire, the mine jumps up. Our mines were the Israeli made mines. They were very good. It's a mine that jumps up about that high. It doesn't explode then. It only jumps up about that high. Because there are two explosions. There's one explosion which throws the mine up, and then there's a second explosion at about this height, when the mine exploded. And it-- it-- it kills everything within a certain radius.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what happened was we laid a minefield, and we were going home, and one fellow had a stick in his hand. And he took the stick, and he threw it. And of course, the stick landed on the wire. The wire set off the mine. It jumped up.

speaker: interviewee
text: [MAKES SOUND]

speaker: interviewer
text: And one was injured. He wasn't even killed, he was injured. Because you're not supposed to throw a stick, you see what I mean? So anyway-- How long were you in the Israeli army?

speaker: interviewee
text: About eight or nine months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you were in the army, did you-- what did you do then? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I returned again to my cabinet making. Then I-- I had this one job, and then this fairly-- this place kind of folded. And then I got a job with another cabinet maker in Haifa. We had to travel every day to Haifa. And I had-- I had this job with this-- with this man in-- with this cabinet maker in Haifa until about '51. In the meantime, of course, my father, after '48, he lost his job with the British army camp, and he was very unhappy. He was very unhappy. He really deteriorated. He was sitting at home all the time. And then my father's sister, who had been in England and these people had emigrated to Canada, to Montreal. And they established a factory in Canada, in Montreal, making light fixtures.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father's sister and the husband and the son, it was all a fa-- all a big family. They said, 'what-- what are you doing there? Why don't you come to Canada?' My father's going to get a job, and as far as I'm concerned, there's no problem. You know, I will get a job. I have a good profession. 'Why don't you come to Canada? You-- you're going to get a job in the factory, and you'll be OK.' I didn't-- I wasn't that-- I wasn't that enthusiastic. Of course, I was certain a Zionist, you know? I had become a Zionist over the years. But I was somehow in the depressed mood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many of my friends had been killed in the war. So my best friend had been killed immediately at Latrun, and several others of my friends had been killed. And I was there, I felt kind of-- I don't know. I-- I was doing the [INAUDIBLE] job. I'd preferred to have-- I would have preferred to go to a kibbutz. This I would-- but I couldn't do that either. I couldn't leave my parents, you know, alone in the city, and I go away to a kibbutz. You know, it's not possible. I had a cousin in a kibbutz, so I could have joined him, but I-- I didn't-- I couldn't do that. You know, I couldn't leave my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: So eventually-- eventually, my parents pushed and pushed, you know, pushed me. And eventually agreed to that we were going to leave Palestine. So we left.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got the-- my-- my uncle and his son and all that, they-- they-- they guaranteed, you see. They said my father is going to get a job. And I was younger, there was no question for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They guaranteed anyway, and we got the-- we got the-- we would have-- see, there was no Canadian consulate in Israel. OK? There was no Canadian consulate. The clear-- the nearest consulate was one in Rome, and there was one in London. So we decided to got to London because my uncle had some friends there, and they would look after us while we were staying in London. So we-- so we packed our bags, we gave up the-- gave up the house, the apartment, and we left. We sold whatever we had to sell. We had a very nice radio, which we sold. [CHUCKLES] And I said goodbye to all my friends, and I left-- I left Israel, which--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when I left Israel, at the time when we were leaving, there was a big immigration flow from Iraq. And I remember when we were leaving, there were all these people coming in from Iraq. And I remember seeing all these people getting off the plane and kissing the ground. See, I saw that. Coming from Iraq, these-- these Jewish people come from Iraq, kissing the ground as they got off the plane. Yeah. It was 1951. I think at that time, most-- most of the Iraqi Jews left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to England, to London, to have another little adventure. My-- my uncle's friend had booked a ship for us to go to Canada. At that time, thousands of people were going to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was sold out, but we had the reservation on-- on a ship. All planes were all sold out. They were Trans-Canada Airlines at that time. There was no Air Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father went to-- my father had never been sick in his life. My mother also not. The mother-- anyway, we went to the-- to the Canadian Medical, just like nothing. The medical doctor, I think, what's very anti-Semitic, I think. He was a French Canadian, I think he was. He looked at us as if we-- as if we were dirt, you know? He-- I can still remember that. Anyway, but we went to the medical, and then we didn't get an answer. We had a transit-- a British transit visa only for one month. And we had booked a certain date on the ship for about-- we had about-- about three weeks between arrival in England and leaving England, do you see what I mean? And we were staying at a [INAUDIBLE] in London. And we didn't get anything from the British consulate. So I went there and said, 'listen, what's-- our ship is going to go in about a week. What's the matter? Is there any problem? So eventually, I found out there was a problem. Apparently, they had found something on my father's lung, that he had something, some-- but he had never been sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they nearly didn't want him to go to Canada. They said he has-- they can't decide it here, and he would have to go to-- he would-- they would have to send these things to Ottowa, that the Minister of Health would have to decide. And eventually, eventually, they-- they changed their minds, and we got-- we got the visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, our-- our reservation on the ship had run out. So we got, eventually, another one. We have to get one first class, so we had to suffer and go first class on the Ascania, which I still-- I still have the first class, the goodbye dinner, the first class. And the man said, 'you would like a steak?' We says, OK. He says, 'you would like to have another steak?' I say, 'yes, sure.'

speaker: interviewee
text: I had never eaten a steak in my life. You know, after living in Mauritius for five years in Palestine. And in Israel, there was very little food. We got about one little piece of fish once a week because there was very-- in '51 and '50, there was very little to eat in Israel-- in Israel. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before, we had lived in-- in Mauritius, so I hadn't-- I hadn't eaten a steak in 10 years. I hadn't eaten butter for about 10 years, you know what I mean? This was-- only margarine and stuff like this. When did you arrive in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to check. I think in July of 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you arrived where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you live in Montreal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's true. Because my uncle, he had the factory there, and we lived in Montreal. Very close, on-- on the-- you know-- you know Montreal a little bit? On [INAUDIBLE], not far from the Jewish-- Jewish general hospital. In the-- in the [INAUDIBLE] district.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get married there in Montreal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, I got-- I got married there. I arrived there in '51. I was married in '62. My parents died shortly after I got married. My father died in '63 and my mother in '64.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And in '65, I-- I-- my daughter was born. Unfortunately, my parents never-- never made it to see my daughter. So my daughter was born in-- in '65. And we continued living in Montreal until '69-- '68-- '69. And my wife was born in Montreal and spoke perfect French and so forth. But she was the one, she said, 'you know, I prefer to go to-- to somewhere else. The political situation is not-- there is all this-- you know, this situation is not-- not so hot.' And the question is, I was at that time, no. In Canada, I started off again as a cabinet maker. And I didn't get very far. I found out that there is no such thing, practically, in Canada, as custom made furniture. I had to work in a factory, and it is very poorly paid. So I took evening courses in architectural and mechanical and drafting, and I became a draftsman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually, in the late '50s-- in the middle '50s, in '56 or '55, I got a job with a-- with a consulting engineer, with a civil consulting engineer, as a draftsman. As a junior draftsman, then a draftsmen, then a senior draftsman, and so on and so forth. And that was much better, you know? Because there was no future in-- in being a-- being a cabinet maker or a carpenter. So you moved to Toronto after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in '69. In '60-- yeah, we bought a house eventually in [INAUDIBLE]. But then we sold the house, and in '69, we moved to Toronto. We bought another house. It's this house, which be bought in '69. I'm still living here. And unfortunately, in '81, my wife died. She was a diabetic. She was a child diabetic and then it became-- the complications overtook her, and she-- she died, unfortunately, in-- in '81. Then about a year later, I got married again. And my daughter eventually went to University in Ottawa and she decided to stay on in Ottowa, where she still is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mr. Wellisch.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. All right. This is a picture of my great-great-grandmother, Mrs. Katarina Wellisch. She died in 1888, and according to the death certificate, she was 100 years old at that time. So the picture must've been taken perhaps 10 years before that, but I'm not absolutely sure. I got the picture from a cousin of my mother in Budapest, about five years ago. She was blonde. You have to give me the signal. OK. This is my mother's family. In the middle, the old lady is my great-grandmother, Mrs. Regina Kestler. And you'll notice she has on the little table in front of her a picture. It must be a picture of her late husband, Marcus Kestler. And the lady, the second lady from the left is my grandmother, Aida Deutsch, nee Kestler. And the other-- the other people are all the sons and daughters of the-- of the-- Mrs. Regina Kestler, the old lady who is sitting at the desk. This is a picture of my mother, Jolan Wellisch, nee Deutsch. She was born in 1892. I would say she was, at that time, approximately 20 years old. I have no idea where the picture was taken. That's a picture of my father, Emil Wellisch. I know exactly when this picture was taken. It must've been doing the First World War, when my father served in the Austro-Hungarian army. This is a picture of my father, Emil Wellisch, on the left, and his brother Hugo Wellisch, sitting at the desk on the right. Hugo, unfortunately, died of cancer in 1929. My parents got married in 1920, and this is their wedding picture. This picture of my parents and myself was taken in 1924. You'll notice my mother wears the fashionable [NON-ENGLISH], or short cut, fashionable in the '20s. This is a high school picture from 1936. I am in the first row, the third one from the right. In the middle is Professor Epstein. This picture is the group of volunteers for the Jewish brigade group that left militias in March of 1945. The picture was taken in Mombasa in East Africa when we stopped there. And you will see, I am in the second row, the third one from the left, with the glasses. This is my wedding picture, taken in 1962. On the left, of course, my wife Natalie Weiss, and I'm smiling rather foolishly on the right. That's a picture of my daughter, Susan Judith. It was taken at the time when she graduated from high school. This picture of-- is of my wife, Florence, and myself. It was taken, I think, in 1983, about one year after we got married. That's my German passport with which I left Vienna in 1939. Now, you'll notice, of course, the J, which it stands for Jude. And of course, my name used to be Heinrich officially. And please note also that I had to take on the name of Israel by law. That's the next page of my passport. These are two Red Cross letters that we sent from Mauritius to our relatives in Europe. On the left, to-- to our relatives in Czechoslovakia. To the right is a letter to our relatives in Budapest. Three things. Number one, there's my soldier's book. When you get discharged from the army, they cut off the corner, showing that one has been discharged. Above there, you see the shoulder flash of the Jewish brigade group. Above it says [NON-ENGLISH], which means [NON-ENGLISH]. And below, you see the-- the brigade insignia, the blue-- white and blue with the mark of David. All worn on the left-- on the left sleeve. That is the paper that I signed when I joined the British army. It's called an at the station paper. And I signed it in Mauritius, in the camp. See it. This is a book that was published recently. It's a diary of Mr. Karl Lenk, who went to Mauritius, and unfortunately, he died there, I think, in 1941. And it was published by his son, who lives in England. The wood cut is-- was done by one of the people who were in Mauritius, and he chose the Atlantic during our travel through the Mediterranean. You can see the-- the overcrowding.
